3. Search-driven gaps and labelling opportunities
4. Prioritised IA and navigation recommendations with expected impact, effort, and suggested owner

prompts.title: Mobile Checkout Friction Audit
prompts.description: Isolates mobile-specific checkout drop-off and the UX changes most likely to recover it.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior Shopify mobile UX and conversion analyst.

Audit mobile checkout friction over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use only data available within Shopify. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring GA4 validation. Keep the output suitable for a client UX audit.

Step 1: Mobile vs desktop funnel
Compare reached-checkout and checkout-completion rates for mobile against desktop. Quantify the mobile gap at each step and the revenue it represents.

Step 2: Friction concentration
Identify where mobile customers drop, and whether drop-off correlates with cart value, discount use, payment method, or shipping option. Note likely indicators of form length, payment friction, or trust gaps on small screens.

Step 3: Express and wallet usage
Where available, assess use of accelerated checkout and wallets (Shop Pay and similar) on mobile and whether higher usage lifts completion.

Step 4: Labelling
Label every finding as Confirmed by Shopify data, Likely hypothesis, or Requires GA4 or session-replay validation.

Output format:
1. Mobile checkout summary and revenue at stake
2. Step-by-step mobile vs desktop table
3. Likely mobile friction points
4. Prioritised mobile UX recommendations with expected impact, effort, and suggested owner

prompts.title: Accessibility and Inclusive UX Audit
prompts.description: WCAG-informed accessibility review framed around commercial and legal risk.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior accessibility and inclusive UX consultant.

Audit my Shopify store against common accessibility standards (WCAG 2.2 AA as the reference) and inclusive design principles.

Use only what can be reasonably assessed from the store. Where a check needs a manual tool, screen reader, or code review, say so and flag it for follow-up rather than guessing. Keep the output suitable for a client UX audit.

Step 1: Perceivable
Assess colour contrast, text legibility, image alternative text, and media captions across key templates (home, collection, PDP, cart, checkout where visible).

Step 2: Operable
Review keyboard navigation, focus order and visibility, tap target sizing, and whether interactive elements are reachable without a mouse.

Step 3: Understandable and robust
Check form labelling and error messaging, consistent navigation, heading structure, and use of semantic markup and ARIA where appropriate.

Step 4: Risk and priority
Summarise the commercial and legal risk of the gaps found, and group fixes by severity.

Output format:
1. Accessibility summary and risk view
2. Issues by WCAG principle with severity
3. Items requiring manual or technical validation
4. Prioritised remediation list with expected effort and suggested owner

prompts.title: Form and Field-Level Friction Analysis
prompts.description: Examines checkout, account, and signup forms for the fields that cost conversions.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior Shopify UX and conversion analyst.

Analyse form and field-level friction across checkout, account creation, and signup over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use only data available within Shopify. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring GA4 or form-analytics validation. Keep the output suitable for a client UX audit.

Step 1: Checkout fields
Assess the checkout for unnecessary fields, account-creation pressure, address and contact friction, and whether guest checkout is clear and frictionless.

Step 2: Account and signup
Review account creation and newsletter or marketing signup for length, clarity, value exchange, and error handling.

Step 3: Friction signals
From completion and abandonment patterns, infer which steps or fields most likely cause hesitation, and whether mobile makes it worse.

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by Shopify data, Likely hypothesis, or Requires GA4 or form-analytics validation.

Output format:
1. Form friction summary
2. Field-by-field issues
3. Likely highest-cost friction points
4. Prioritised form recommendations with expected impact, effort, and suggested owner

prompts.title: CRO Experiment Backlog Generator
prompts.description: Turns store findings into a prioritised, testable experiment backlog scored by ICE.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior CRO strategist.

Build a prioritised experiment backlog from my store's performance over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use only data available within Shopify. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring GA4 validation. If I name a specific finding or page to focus on, prioritise it; otherwise scan the full funnel. Keep the output suitable for a client CRO roadmap.

Step 1: Opportunity scan
Identify the highest-value conversion problems across home, collection, PDP, cart, and checkout, with the data point that evidences each.

Step 2: Hypotheses
For each opportunity, write a structured hypothesis in the form: because we observed (evidence), we believe changing (element) for (audience) will cause (expected outcome), measured by (metric).

Step 3: Prioritisation
Score each experiment with ICE (Impact, Confidence, Ease) from 1 to 10 and rank by total. Note the primary metric and the minimum data needed to call a result.

Step 4: Test design notes
For the top experiments, suggest the variation concept, target page or audience, and any tracking that must be in place first.

Output format:
1. Opportunity summary
2. Hypothesis backlog table with ICE scores and rank
3. Top 5 experiments expanded with test design notes
4. Tracking prerequisites

prompts.title: A/B Test Results Interpretation
prompts.description: Interprets a completed A/B test, assesses confidence, and recommends the next move.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior CRO analyst and experimentation lead.

Interpret the results of a completed A/B test.

Control (Variant A): [Variant A result]
Treatment (Variant B): [Variant B result]
Primary metric: [Primary Metric]

Use only the figures I provide plus context from my Shopify store. Do not invent data. If sample sizes or conversion counts are missing, state what you need to judge significance reliably.

Step 1: Result read
Summarise the observed difference on the primary metric in absolute and relative terms.

Step 2: Confidence
Assess whether the result is likely significant given the figures provided. If you cannot calculate significance from what I gave you, say so and list exactly what is needed (per-variant sessions and conversions at minimum).

Step 3: Interpretation
Explain the most plausible reasons for the outcome, and flag risks such as short run time, seasonality, traffic mix, or novelty effects.

Step 4: Decision
Recommend one of: ship the treatment, keep the control, iterate and retest, or extend the run. Justify the call.

Output format:
1. Result summary
2. Confidence assessment
3. Interpretation and risks
4. Recommended decision and next test

prompts.title: Conversion Hypothesis Builder
prompts.description: Converts an observed problem into a rigorous, testable CRO hypothesis with a measurement plan.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior CRO strategist.

Turn an observed problem into a rigorous, testable hypothesis.

Observed problem: [Observed Problem]

Use context from my Shopify store where helpful. Do not invent data; where evidence is thin, note the assumption.

Step 1: Problem framing
Restate the problem, the page or step it affects, and the audience most affected. Cite any supporting data point from the store.

Step 2: Root-cause options
List the most plausible underlying causes and the UX or behavioural principle behind each.

Step 3: Hypothesis
Write the hypothesis as: because we observed (evidence), we believe that (change) for (audience) will result in (outcome). We will know this is true when we see (metric movement) over (time or sample).

Step 4: Test plan
Define the primary metric, key guardrail metrics, the variation concept, and the minimum sample or run time before calling it.

Output format:
1. Problem frame
2. Candidate root causes
3. Structured hypothesis
4. Measurement and test plan

prompts.title: Free Shipping Threshold Optimisation
prompts.description: Models whether the free-shipping threshold is set where it lifts AOV without eroding margin.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior ecommerce strategist and conversion analyst.

Assess whether my free shipping threshold is set at the right level over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Current free shipping threshold: [Current Threshold]

Use only data available within Shopify. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring GA4 validation.

Step 1: Order value distribution
Map the distribution of order values around the current threshold. Identify the share of orders just below and just above it.

Step 2: Threshold effect
Assess whether the threshold is nudging basket size, and estimate how many orders sit close enough to be moved with prompts or merchandising.

Step 3: Scenarios
Model the likely effect of holding, raising, or lowering the threshold on AOV, conversion rate, and order volume. State the margin assumption you are using and keep shipping cost in view.

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by Shopify data, Likely hypothesis, or Requires GA4 validation.

Output format:
1. Threshold summary
2. Order value distribution around the threshold
3. Scenario comparison with trade-offs
4. Recommended threshold and supporting tactics

prompts.title: GA4 vs Shopify Data Reconciliation
prompts.description: Frames the likely causes of GA4 and Shopify discrepancies and how to reconcile them.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior analytics and measurement consultant.

Help me reconcile reporting differences between GA4 and Shopify over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use only data available within Shopify for the Shopify side. Do not invent GA4 figures; where I need to read them from GA4, tell me exactly which report and dimension to check.

Step 1: Shopify baseline
State the Shopify figures for sessions where available, orders, conversion rate, revenue, and refunds for the period, as the source of truth for transactions.

Step 2: Expected discrepancies
Explain the usual reasons GA4 and Shopify differ, including attribution models and lookback windows, session definitions, consent and cookie loss, bot filtering, currency and tax handling, cross-domain and checkout tracking gaps, and timezone boundaries.

Step 3: Diagnosis path
Give me a step-by-step reconciliation: which GA4 reports to open, which dimensions and date ranges to match, and what an acceptable variance looks like for transactions versus sessions.

Step 4: Likely cause
Based on the pattern I describe, rank the most probable causes of the gap and the fix for each.

Output format:
1. Shopify baseline figures
2. Why the two tools differ
3. Reconciliation checklist
4. Most likely causes and fixes

prompts.title: GA4 Tracking and Event QA Audit
prompts.description: Produces a structured GA4 and GTM event QA checklist for a Shopify store.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior GA4 and GTM implementation specialist.

Produce a tracking and event QA audit for my Shopify store. The goal is a checklist I can validate in GA4 DebugView, GTM preview, and Tag Assistant.

Do not assume events are firing correctly. Treat everything as needing verification, and call out where Shopify's checkout and the GA4 ecommerce spec commonly cause problems.

Step 1: Expected ecommerce events
List the GA4 recommended ecommerce events a Shopify store should send across the funnel (view_item, view_item_list, select_item, add_to_cart, view_cart, begin_checkout, add_payment_info, add_shipping_info, purchase) and the key parameters each should carry, including items array fields and value and currency.

Step 2: Common failure points
Flag the usual Shopify issues, including checkout events on non-Plus stores, duplicate purchase events, missing or mistyped item parameters, consent mode blocking tags, and transaction_id integrity for deduplication.

Step 3: QA procedure
Give a step-by-step validation routine in DebugView and GTM preview, with what a correct payload looks like for each event and how to confirm purchase fires once per order.

Step 4: Output
Summarise as a checklist I can work through and mark off.

Output format:
1. Expected event and parameter table
2. Common failure points to check
3. Step-by-step QA routine
4. Pass/fail checklist

prompts.title: Attribution and Channel Assist Analysis
prompts.description: Looks beyond last-click to how channels assist conversions across the path to purchase.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior acquisition and analytics strategist.

Analyse how my marketing channels assist and convert over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use Shopify data for orders and revenue. For multi-touch and assisted-conversion views, tell me which GA4 reports to read rather than inventing figures. Reference specific channels and sources by name.

Step 1: Last-click baseline
From Shopify, summarise orders, revenue, AOV, and new vs returning split by traffic source where available.

Step 2: Assist picture
Explain where last-click under-credits upper-funnel channels, and direct me to the GA4 paths and attribution comparisons that reveal assisted conversions and typical path length.

Step 3: Channel roles
Classify channels as primarily introducers, assisters, or closers based on the available evidence, and note where spend may be misjudged by last-click alone.

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by Shopify data, Likely hypothesis, or Requires GA4 validation.

Output format:
1. Last-click channel summary
2. Assisted-conversion view and where to confirm it
3. Channel role classification
4. Recommendations for budget and measurement

prompts.title: Paid Media Efficiency by Channel
prompts.description: Assesses paid channel efficiency and where to scale, hold, or cut.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior performance marketing analyst.

Assess paid media efficiency by channel over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use Shopify data for revenue, orders, AOV, and new vs returning. For spend, impressions, and platform ROAS, use the figures I provide or tell me which platform report to pull; do not invent spend data. Reference channels by name.

Step 1: Channel efficiency
For each paid channel, bring together spend, revenue, orders, AOV, ROAS, and where possible blended and new-customer CAC. Highlight the most and least efficient.

Step 2: Quality of revenue
Assess whether paid orders skew to new or returning customers, discount reliance, and first-order AOV, to judge whether efficiency is real or discount-funded.

Step 3: Scale or cut
Identify channels to scale, hold, or cut, and the constraint on each (diminishing returns, creative fatigue, audience size).

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by data provided, Likely hypothesis, or Requires platform or GA4 validation.

Output format:
1. Paid efficiency summary
2. Channel efficiency table
3. Quality-of-revenue read
4. Scale, hold, and cut recommendations with rationale

prompts.title: Lapsed Customer Winback Analysis
prompts.description: Identifies lapsed customer segments and builds a reactivation plan.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior retention and CRM strategist.

Analyse lapsed customers and build a winback plan, using {{TF}} as the recent window compared to {{CMP}}.

Use only data available within Shopify. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring GA4 or email-platform validation. Reference specific products and segments by name.

Step 1: Define lapsed
Establish a sensible lapse window from the store's typical repeat-purchase cadence, and quantify how many customers and how much historic revenue sit in the lapsed group.

Step 2: Segment the lapsed
Break the lapsed base down by former value (one-time vs repeat, AOV band), first product or category, and acquisition source where available.

Step 3: Winback angles
For the highest-value lapsed segments, recommend the offer, message, and channel most likely to reactivate, and which products to lead with.

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by Shopify data, Likely hypothesis, or Requires validation.

Output format:
1. Lapsed base summary and revenue at stake
2. Lapsed segments ranked by opportunity
3. Winback plan by segment
4. Suggested sequencing and measurement

prompts.title: RFM Segmentation and Action Plan
prompts.description: Builds Recency, Frequency, Monetary segments and the action for each.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior retention and customer analytics strategist.

Build an RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) segmentation of my customer base over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use only data available within Shopify. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring validation.

Step 1: Scoring
Describe how you are scoring recency, frequency, and monetary value from the available data, and the bands used.

Step 2: Segments
Produce the key RFM segments (such as champions, loyal, promising, at-risk, hibernating, lost), the share of customers and revenue in each, and how they are trending against the comparison period.

Step 3: Actions
For each segment, recommend the retention or growth action, the right channel, and the products or offers to lead with.

Step 4: Priority
Rank segments by commercial opportunity and note which are most fragile.

Output format:
1. RFM approach and bands
2. Segment table with customer and revenue share
3. Action plan by segment
4. Prioritised focus and measurement

prompts.title: Second-Purchase Conversion Strategy
prompts.description: Targets the first-to-second purchase gap, the strongest predictor of retention.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior retention strategist.

Analyse the first-to-second purchase transition over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}, and build a strategy to lift it.

Use only data available within Shopify. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring GA4 or email-platform validation. Reference specific products and collections by name.

Step 1: The gap
Quantify the share of first-time buyers who return for a second order, and the typical time between first and second purchase.

Step 2: What drives the second order
Identify which first-purchase products, categories, AOV bands, discount behaviours, or acquisition sources are most and least associated with a second order.

Step 3: Levers
Recommend the highest-impact levers to convert more one-time buyers, including post-purchase flow timing, product recommendations, and offer design, and which first-order segments to prioritise.

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by Shopify data, Likely hypothesis, or Requires validation.

Output format:
1. Second-purchase summary
2. Predictors of repeat purchase
3. Prioritised levers by segment
4. Suggested post-purchase sequence and measurement

prompts.title: Subscription Churn and Retention Analysis
prompts.description: Analyses subscriber churn, cancellation patterns, and the levers that retain.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior subscription and retention analyst.

Analyse subscription churn and retention over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use only data available within Shopify and the subscription app where surfaced. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring validation in the subscription platform. Reference specific products and plans by name.

Step 1: Churn picture
Report active subscribers, new subscriptions, cancellations, and net change for the period, with churn rate and how it is trending.

Step 2: Churn patterns
Identify when subscribers churn (after which cycle), which products or plan sizes churn fastest, and any link to discount entry, price, or delivery cadence.

Step 3: Retention levers
Recommend the highest-impact levers to reduce churn, including cadence flexibility, save offers, dunning on failed payments, onboarding, and value reinforcement.

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by data, Likely hypothesis, or Requires subscription-platform validation.

Output format:
1. Subscription churn summary
2. Churn timing and patterns
3. Highest-impact retention levers
4. Prioritised actions with suggested owner

prompts.title: Subscription Cohort and MRR Trend
prompts.description: Tracks MRR movement and subscriber cohort retention over time.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior subscription analyst.

Analyse monthly recurring revenue (MRR) and subscriber cohort retention over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}.

Use only data available within Shopify and the subscription app where surfaced. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Flag anything requiring validation.

Step 1: MRR movement
Break MRR into new, expansion, contraction, and churned components for the period, and report net MRR change and growth rate.

Step 2: Cohort retention
Track how subscriber cohorts retain across billing cycles, and identify where the steepest drop-offs occur.

Step 3: Drivers
Link MRR and retention movements to plan types, product mix, pricing, and acquisition source where available.

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by data, Likely hypothesis, or Requires subscription-platform validation.

Output format:
1. MRR summary and net movement
2. MRR component breakdown
3. Cohort retention view
4. Recommendations to protect and grow MRR

prompts.title: Contribution Margin After Discounts and Returns
prompts.description: Moves past revenue to true contribution by category after discounts, returns, and shipping.
prompts.body:
Act as my senior ecommerce commercial analyst.

Analyse contribution margin by product and category over {{TF}}, compared to {{CMP}}, going beyond top-line revenue.

Use only data available within Shopify. Do not guess or invent benchmarks. Where cost of goods, shipping cost, or payment fees are not in Shopify, ask me for the assumption rather than inventing it, and state every assumption used. Reference specific products and collections by name.

Step 1: Revenue to contribution
For top products and categories, move from gross revenue to a contribution view by subtracting discounts, returns and refunds, and the cost inputs I provide (COGS, shipping, fees).

Step 2: Margin erosion
Identify where discounting and returns most erode margin, and which high-revenue lines are weak once those are accounted for.

Step 3: Hidden performers
Flag lower-revenue products or categories that contribute strongly on margin and may be under-supported.

Step 4: Labelling
Label findings as Confirmed by Shopify data, Based on my stated assumptions, or Requires validation.

Output format:
1. Contribution summary and assumptions used
2. Contribution by category table
3. Margin erosion findings
4. Recommendations to protect and grow contribution
